sources: Dr. Mark Hyman — The Pegan Diet, The Doctor's Farmacy, Ultra-Inflammation framework
Dr. Emeran Mayer — The Mind-Gut Connection (2016)
Giulia Enders — Gut: The Inside Story (2014)
Dr. Mark Stengler — The Holistic Guide to Gut Health
Peer-reviewed literature on oleocanthal, polyphenols, gut-thyroid axis, SCFAs
meta.note: These protocols are filtered for real-world applicability — office environment, international travel, family with kids, no private chef. Ranked by evidence strength AND practical achievability for Randy's lifestyle.

core_principle: Inflammation is the common thread between Hashimoto's autoimmunity, accelerated aging, mood dysregulation, metabolic dysfunction, and poor recovery. Every intervention below addresses inflammation through a specific, evidence-based mechanism. Prioritize interventions with the highest impact-to-effort ratio given Randy's real-world constraints.

leaky_gut_hashimotos_connection.summary: The gut-thyroid axis is one of the most evidence-supported mechanisms in autoimmune thyroid disease. Understanding it makes Randy's current interventions make more sense and identifies the next highest-leverage additions.
mechanism: 1. Gluten (and other gut irritants) trigger release of ZONULIN — a protein that opens tight junctions between intestinal cells
2. Open tight junctions = 'leaky gut' = bacterial antigens, undigested food particles, and LPS (lipopolysaccharide) enter the bloodstream
3. Immune system activates against these foreign particles
4. MOLECULAR MIMICRY: thyroid tissue shares structural similarities with certain food proteins. Immune response targeting foreign antigens can cross-react with thyroid tissue → TPOAb elevation
5. Dysbiosis (poor gut microbiome diversity) amplifies this cascade — fewer beneficial bacteria = less SCFA production = weaker gut barrier integrity
6. Result: chronic autoimmune signaling even when gluten is removed, until gut barrier fully heals and microbiome diversity is restored
leaky_gut_hashimotos_connection.randy_relevance: Randy's gluten-free diet directly addresses step 1 (zonulin trigger). The gut microbiome restoration work (fermented foods, fiber diversity, prebiotics) addresses steps 4-6. This is why gut health recommendations are not tangential — they are core to TPOAb reduction.
leaky_gut_hashimotos_connection.healing_timeline: Gut barrier restoration typically requires 3-12 months of consistent intervention. TPOAb may continue to be elevated while the gut heals even if the gluten trigger is removed. This context helps explain why the first lab retest may not show dramatic TPOAb change yet.

evoo_oleocanthal.title: High-Polyphenol Extra Virgin Olive Oil (Oleocanthal)
mechanism: Oleocanthal inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes — the same pathways as ibuprofen — suppressing prostaglandin synthesis and systemic inflammation
NF-κB pathway suppression: reduces expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, CRP)
TRPA1 receptor activation in the pharynx produces the characteristic 'throat catch' — a reliable quality proxy
Additional polyphenols (hydroxytyrosol, oleuropein) provide antioxidant and gut-barrier protective effects
Polyphenols act as prebiotics — feeding beneficial gut bacteria and increasing SCFA production

quality_metric.cough_test: Swallow 1 tsp straight. A distinct catch/sting at the back of the throat (not the mouth) indicates meaningful oleocanthal. 'One-cough' = moderate; 'two-cough' = high quality.
quality_metric.harvest_date: Look for harvest date on label (not just 'best by'). Use within 12-18 months of harvest.
quality_metric.storage: Dark glass bottle, cool/dark location, away from heat and direct light. Polyphenols degrade significantly with heat and light exposure.
quality_metric.avoid: Light/clear glass bottles, bottles without harvest date, 'light' olive oil, blends labeled only 'Product of multiple countries' without harvest info

evoo_oleocanthal.dose: 1-2 tablespoons per meal. Bryan Johnson uses 1 tbsp per meal (3 meals = 3 tbsp/day). Even 1-2 tbsp/day delivers meaningful anti-inflammatory effect.
practical_for_randy: At home: easy swap — buy high-polyphenol EVOO instead of standard
Traveling: most decent restaurants use olive oil — ask for EVOO and extra on the side
Office lunch: drizzle on salad, grain bowls, anything available
Family: completely compatible — kids can eat it too, it just replaces other oils
evoo_oleocanthal.hashimoto_relevance: Reduces the systemic inflammatory burden that drives TPOAb elevation. Supports gut barrier integrity through prebiotic polyphenol effect.

fiber_diversity.title: Dietary Fiber Diversity (Bulsiewicz / Fiber Fueled framework)
mechanism: Diverse fiber types feed different bacterial species → greater microbiome diversity
Fiber fermentation produces Short-Chain Fatty Acids (SCFAs): butyrate, propionate, acetate
Butyrate is the primary fuel for colonocytes (gut lining cells) — directly repairs and maintains gut barrier integrity
SCFA production reduces intestinal permeability ('leaky gut') — directly addresses the Hashimoto's cascade
Diverse microbiome = stronger immune regulation = reduced autoimmune signaling
fiber_diversity.target: 30 different plant foods per week (Bulsiewicz's research-backed target). Each unique plant = unique fiber + polyphenol profile feeding different bacterial species.
randy_already_doing: Chia seeds in shakes — soluble fiber, omega-3, prebiotic
Hemp hearts in shakes — protein + omega-3 + fiber
Flax seeds in shakes — lignan polyphenols, soluble fiber, anti-inflammatory omega-3 (ALA)
easy_additions: Berries daily (blueberry, raspberry, blackberry) — prebiotic fiber + polyphenols
Garlic and onion in cooking — inulin prebiotic fiber, feeds Bifidobacterium
Lentils or chickpeas 2-3x/week — also hits protein target for weight gain
Leafy greens (spinach, arugula, kale) — easy to add to any meal
Sauerkraut or kimchi (1-2 tbsp/day) — fermented + fiber + probiotics combined
practical_for_randy: Family meals: add lentils to soups/stews, nobody notices
Travel: berries available everywhere, order extra vegetables at restaurants
Office: nuts, seeds, and fruit are portable — doesn't require meal prep
fiber_diversity.hashimoto_relevance: SCFA production from diverse fiber is the single most evidence-supported mechanism for gut barrier repair — directly addressing the leaky gut → TPOAb cascade.

fermented_foods.title: Fermented Foods (Live Cultures)
fermented_foods.evidence: strong — Stanford 2021 RCT (Wastyk et al.) showed 10-week high-fermented-food diet increased microbiome diversity AND reduced inflammatory markers including IL-6
mechanism: Live bacteria directly colonize the gut temporarily, supporting diversity
Fermentation pre-digests food compounds, improving bioavailability
Produces beneficial metabolites including SCFA precursors and B vitamins
Reduces pathogenic bacterial overgrowth through competitive exclusion
sources: Kimchi — also anti-inflammatory from garlic/ginger/capsaicin; gluten-free; travel-packaged versions exist
Sauerkraut — simplest, just fermented cabbage; gluten-free; long shelf life
Coconut yogurt (dairy-free) — probiotic cultures without lactose issue
Water kefir (dairy-free alternative to milk kefir)
Miso paste — note: contains soy; use in cooking for flavor + probiotics (heat kills bacteria, so add at end)
fermented_foods.dose: 1-2 servings per day. Consistency over quantity.
practical_for_randy: 1-2 tbsp sauerkraut or kimchi alongside any meal — takes 10 seconds
Travel: shelf-stable kimchi packets, coconut yogurt available at most airports/hotels
Family: easy to keep a jar of sauerkraut in fridge — kids often ignore it, doesn't affect family meals
fermented_foods.hashimoto_relevance: Microbiome diversity is directly inversely correlated with autoimmune disease severity. The Stanford study specifically showed reduced IL-6, which drives thyroid autoimmunity.

omega3_epa_dha.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids (EPA/DHA)
mechanism: EPA and DHA are direct precursors to resolvins and protectins — pro-resolving mediators that actively switch OFF inflammation (not just reduce it)
Compete with omega-6 arachidonic acid for COX/LOX enzymes — shifting eicosanoid production toward anti-inflammatory forms
Reduce NF-κB activation and TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6 production
Support gut barrier integrity and reduce intestinal permeability
omega3_epa_dha.randy_status: Already taking 1.5-2g EPA/DHA fish oil — this is a solid dose. Consistency (60-70%) is the gap.
omega3_epa_dha.dose: 1-2g EPA/DHA daily minimum. 2-4g in the context of active autoimmunity or high inflammation.
omega3_epa_dha.hashimoto_relevance: Omega-3s directly reduce the Th17/Th1 immune imbalance characteristic of Hashimoto's autoimmunity.

polyphenol_diversity.title: Polyphenol Diversity (Hyman / Pegan Diet framework)
polyphenol_diversity.evidence: strong — polyphenols are the most studied class of dietary anti-inflammatory compounds
mechanism: Polyphenols modulate NF-κB, the 'master switch' of inflammation
Act as prebiotics — feeding Akkermansia muciniphila and Bifidobacterium species that support gut barrier
Activate Nrf2 pathway — upregulates endogenous antioxidant enzymes (glutathione, SOD, catalase)
Many polyphenols directly modulate immune function and reduce autoimmune signaling

key_compounds_and_sources.anthocyanins: Blueberries, blackberries, raspberries, purple cabbage — most anti-inflammatory berry polyphenols
key_compounds_and_sources.curcumin: Turmeric — requires black pepper (piperine) for 20x bioavailability. Easy to add to shakes or cooking.
key_compounds_and_sources.quercetin: Capers, red onion, apples — also a natural antihistamine and mast cell stabilizer
key_compounds_and_sources.resveratrol: Grapes, blueberries — modest dose in food form
key_compounds_and_sources.EGCG: Green tea — easy daily habit; also anti-inflammatory and modulates thyroid antibodies
key_compounds_and_sources.sulforaphane: Broccoli sprouts (highest source), broccoli, cauliflower — activates Nrf2 powerfully; glucosinolate note: cooking degrades enzyme, eat some raw or lightly steamed

practical_for_randy: Turmeric + black pepper in protein shakes — 1/4 tsp turmeric, pinch of black pepper; no taste impact
Blueberries daily — frozen works as well as fresh for polyphenols
Green tea as a coffee alternative 1-2x/day
Add capers or red onion to whatever meals make sense
Lightly steamed or raw cruciferous vegetables 3-4x/week
polyphenol_diversity.hashimoto_relevance: Curcumin and EGCG have specific evidence for reducing thyroid autoantibodies in small RCTs. NF-κB suppression is the most direct anti-inflammatory mechanism available through food.

gut_brain_axis_mood.title: Gut-Brain Axis (Mayer / Mind-Gut Connection framework)
mechanism: 90% of the body's serotonin is produced in the gut by enterochromaffin cells, not the brain
The enteric nervous system (500M neurons) communicates bidirectionally with the brain via the vagus nerve
Gut microbiome produces neurotransmitter precursors: tryptophan (serotonin), GABA, dopamine precursors
Dysbiosis → elevated LPS → neuroinflammation → depression and anxiety
Psychobiotics: specific probiotic strains (Lactobacillus rhamnosus, L. helveticus + B. longum) have RCT evidence for reducing anxiety and cortisol
gut_brain_axis_mood.randy_relevance: The gut-brain connection is directly relevant to the Wellbutrin context. Improving gut microbiome diversity, reducing intestinal permeability, and supporting serotonin precursor availability (via tryptophan-rich foods) all support mood regulation through mechanisms complementary to bupropion. This is the biological basis for lifestyle → mood improvement. NEVER position as replacing medication — frame as supporting the same neurochemical targets through parallel pathways.
practical_additions: Tryptophan-rich foods: turkey, eggs, pumpkin seeds, tofu — support serotonin synthesis
Probiotic strain: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (widely available, Culturelle brand) has the strongest mood evidence
Reducing sugar: sugar feeds pathogenic bacteria, disrupts Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium balance, spikes neuroinflammation
Fermented foods (above) — also support the gut-brain axis via SCFA production

hyman_5_root_causes_autoimmune.title: Hyman's 5 Root Causes of Autoimmune Disease

leaky_gut.status: Being addressed — gluten-free diet, omega-3s, fermented foods protocol
leaky_gut.remaining_gap: Fiber diversity and fermented food consistency could be improved

food_sensitivities.remaining_gap: Other potential sensitivities (nightshades, eggs, soy) are unknown — elimination testing could be done if TPOAb doesn't respond. Not priority yet.

chronic_infections.note: EBV (Epstein-Barr Virus) reactivation is a known Hashimoto's trigger. H. pylori also implicated. Worth flagging for clinician if TPOAb doesn't respond to current interventions.

environmental_toxins.status: Partially addressed — clean diet, no alcohol
environmental_toxins.practical_additions: BPA-free containers (Randy likely already uses), filtered water, minimize processed food packaging. Sauna sweating for toxin elimination (if VASA has one) has some evidence.

chronic_stress.status: Partially addressed via exercise and sleep
chronic_stress.note: Cortisol directly suppresses regulatory T cells and amplifies autoimmune activity. HRV as tracked by Oura is the best available proxy for autonomic stress load.

anti_inflammatory_spices.title: Anti-Inflammatory Spices (High Impact, Zero Effort)
anti_inflammatory_spices.evidence: strong for curcumin; moderate for others

turmeric_curcumin.dose: 1/2-1 tsp turmeric + pinch of black pepper (piperine increases bioavailability 20x)
turmeric_curcumin.mechanism: NF-κB inhibition, IL-6 and TNF-α reduction, direct antioxidant
turmeric_curcumin.how: Add to protein shakes — completely undetectable in taste. Or cooking.
turmeric_curcumin.hashimoto_note: Small RCTs show curcumin reduces thyroid autoantibody levels

ginger.mechanism: COX inhibition (like oleocanthal), NF-κB suppression, gut motility support
ginger.how: Fresh ginger to shakes or tea; powdered in cooking

cinnamon.mechanism: Improves insulin sensitivity (blood glucose regulation — relevant to Randy's glucose flag), mild anti-inflammatory

anti_inflammatory_spices.practical_for_randy: All of these can go into a protein shake. 30 seconds, no extra meal prep, family doesn't need to change anything.

sleep_inflammation_connection.title: Sleep Quality as Anti-Inflammatory Intervention
mechanism: During deep sleep: growth hormone released → tissue repair, muscle protein synthesis
Glymphatic system active: cerebrospinal fluid flushes brain — removes inflammatory metabolites including beta-amyloid
Below 7 hours: significant increase in CRP, IL-6, TNF-α within days
Poor sleep → elevated cortisol → cortisol drives NF-κB → amplifies autoimmune activity
Oura data makes this directly trackable: HRV as the best single proxy for systemic inflammation and recovery
sleep_inflammation_connection.randy_context: Eszopiclone supports sleep onset but may reduce deep and REM sleep quality at higher doses. The goal of reducing eszopiclone over time, combined with magnesium glycinate, consistent bedtime, LDN timing optimization, and reduced inflammation, is directly tied to improving sleep architecture — which then reduces systemic inflammation, supports TPOAb reduction, and supports mood.

practical_targets.bedtime_consistency: Same time ±30 min is more important than total hours
practical_targets.realistic_target: 10:00-10:30 PM (not 8:30 PM — this isn't realistic with family/kids)
practical_targets.no_food: 2-3 hours before bed minimum — large meals raise HR and disrupt HRV
practical_targets.wind_down: 30-45 min dimmed lights, no work email — family permitting
practical_targets.magnesium: Glycinate form at bedtime — supports GABA and parasympathetic tone, promotes deep sleep

sauna_protocol.title: Dry Sauna (Bryan Johnson Protocol)
sauna_protocol.evidence: strong — Finnish cohort studies, Bryan Johnson hsCRP results
mechanism: Heat shock proteins: activated by sauna → cellular repair and protein quality control
Hormesis: controlled heat stress → adaptive anti-inflammatory response
Toxin elimination via sweat: 15 sessions showed 65% reduction in 2,4-D, 100% elimination of MEHP and perchlorate
Cardiovascular: Bryan saw 12.6% reduction in central blood pressure after 23 sessions
Bryan's hsCRP dropped to below detectable levels (combined with other interventions)
sauna_protocol.protocol: 175°F (80°C), 15-20 minutes, 2-3x/week post-workout (not before, as it causes significant fatigue)
sauna_protocol.randy_practical: If VASA has a sauna, this is a zero-incremental-cost intervention — just extend post-workout by 20 min, 2-3 days/week. High impact for Hashimoto's inflammation and cardiovascular health.
sauna_protocol.hashimoto_relevance: hsCRP reduction is the most direct measurable anti-inflammatory outcome — relevant to both autoimmune activity and aging.

evoo_practical_sourcing.title: How to Buy High-Polyphenol EVOO in the Real World
what_to_look_for: Total biophenols listed on label: target >400 mg/kg (some will say '500+' or '750+')
Harvest date on label (month/year) — not just 'best by' date
Single origin or single estate (better traceability and consistency)
'Early harvest' or 'Novembre' on label — picked when olives are green/immature, highest polyphenol content
Koroneiki, Picual, or Coratina variety listed — these are the highest polyphenol cultivars
recommended_brands_usa: Myrolion — Greek Koroneiki, lab-verified 500-750+ mg/kg, order online
Kosterina — Greek-American, widely available at Whole Foods and Amazon, ~400-600 mg/kg
Mina — Moroccan Picholine variety, available at Costco and Amazon, very good value
Zoe — Spanish Picual, available at Whole Foods
Olio Carli (Italian) — high quality, direct subscription available
evoo_practical_sourcing.travel_use: At restaurants: ask for EVOO specifically and request extra on the side for drizzling. Italian, Mediterranean, and Greek restaurants almost always have quality EVOO.
evoo_practical_sourcing.avoid: Anything in clear/plastic bottles, 'pure olive oil' or 'light olive oil' (refined, low polyphenols), generic supermarket brands without harvest date

practical_priority_list_for_randy.description: Ranked by impact × feasibility for someone with Randy's specific profile (Hashimoto's, office job, family, international travel). These are not theoretical — these are the highest-leverage moves.
tier1_do_now: Upgrade to high-polyphenol EVOO (Kosterina or Mina widely available) — one purchase, used daily
Add turmeric + black pepper to daily protein shakes — 30 seconds, zero lifestyle impact
Add 1-2 tbsp sauerkraut or kimchi alongside one meal per day — easy, affordable
Use VASA sauna 2-3x/week post-workout if available — zero extra cost
tier2_build_habit: Target 30 different plant foods per week — track casually, include spices and herbs as individual plants
Add green tea 1-2x/day (mornings work) — easy caffeine-moderate alternative
Increase supplement consistency to 85%+ — the checklist in TrainAI addresses this directly
Consistent 10-10:30 PM bedtime — one of the highest ROI sleep interventions
tier3_when_ready: Add specific probiotic strain (Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG / Culturelle) for gut-brain and microbiome support
Consider ginger supplement or daily fresh ginger addition to shakes
Explore CGM (continuous glucose monitor) 2-week trial — given uncertain fasting glucose result, this would show how Randy's diet affects blood sugar in real time
not_for_randy: Noon last meal — not compatible with family life and work
8:30 PM bedtime — not realistic with kids
Extensive medical procedures (HBOT, stem cells, gene therapy) — Bryan Johnson's extreme tier
Psychedelic therapy — not aligned with Randy's stated preferences
Private-chef-required meal prep — has to work in an office and while traveling

coach_rules: When recommending anti-inflammatory interventions, connect them to Randy's specific health context: Hashimoto's TPOAb reduction, gut barrier repair, mood support (parallel to Wellbutrin), or aging/longevity.
Prioritize high-impact, low-effort interventions that work in offices, restaurants, and with family meals.
Never make Randy feel like he needs a bio-dome to be healthy. 80% of the benefit comes from 20% of the interventions.
When EVOO is mentioned, be specific about polyphenol quality — not just 'use olive oil'.
The gut-Hashimoto's connection makes fiber diversity and fermented foods directly therapeutic, not just nutritional.
Connect sleep quality improvements to both anti-inflammatory outcomes AND mood support — these are not separate goals.
When supplement adherence is low in the log, note the specific mechanisms being missed (e.g., missed fish oil = missed COX pathway anti-inflammatory dose).
